item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I first read this years ago I remember disliking it, mainly because the neo-pronouns put me off from trying the CYOA more than once, and the ending I originally got had no sense of closer, imo. I never downvoted though, cause it didn't feel fair to rate an article based on some distracting word choices (and my beliefs have changed a bit, in any case).

Reading it now… I must have done something wrong years ago cause I thought the werewolves were the cultists until now! And man, did I miss out on what was passed the Gate.

I found this to be a very enjoyable experience. Upvoted wholeheartedly!

And to the folks who felt the CYOA just boiled down to "generic eldritch entity won":

(+ Spoilers)
Welcome Home

In every "…" secret message, the speaker (weather it's the cult leader or the White Sun or whoever) gives their thoughts on the particular ending you reached. In the vast majority, they seem content with what's happened. However, in the endings where you talked to the professor and meet the Hunter, the speaker practically throws a fit; they basically say "NO, I DON'T LIKE THIS ENDING! WAAAAHHHHH!" They also shout the phrase "There is a sun, another sun…" I believe this phrase signifies their discontentment with an ending, and they're trying to hypnotize the narrator into getting a "better" ending. This phrase is also present in the majority of endings where you talk to the professor. Remember what the narrator says if you choose not to talk to her?

"I figured we could just go into the town ourselves and — shut up, I'm talking. I told you I don't care if this matches the other reports or not."

This leads me to believe the task force did talk to her, and in some of the endings that result from this choice the White Sun doesn't win. And in one ending, no eldritch entity wins, which might be a good thing:

"If I'd kept going, like the strange impulse said I should, I don't know what would have happened. Maybe I wouldn't have ever come back — and maybe that would be the fortunate thing to happen. Maybe I'd still be standing before you today same as now, except it wouldn't be me, it would be…

…Yeah, sorry. Christ. Just… a lot of dreams. Really strange dreams… Hard to take. All of this has been hard to take. You know? No, I guess you don't."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This shares the same title as SCP-2108

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read this scip a while back and I am happy to say that it is one of my favorites. Every once in a while it just pops back into my head - the idea of the sun cult, the whole town being in on it, the actual second sun hiding under the earth, and the interactive aspect at the end of the file make it a really interesting story!

Edit: also wanted to add that I love the slow buildup of the article until almost all is revealed in the AAR - its a good storytelling technique!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1
you should probably close this since you don't need to waste time reading other people's subjective opinions

I tried to read it, and I did finally force myself to, but I didn't enjoy it. I only managed b/c I just opened the page source to read the text without the cyoa sh— stuff. Once I reached the gender-redefining pronouns I was especially checking out. But I pushed through b/c I was really hoping for something good in spite of that.

But to quote the previous post

After exhausting each, all I got was "Welp, this generic eldritch entity won. Good for it."

Yep… nooooooooooooooooooooooooooope.

This feels like an exercise in alternative gender inclusivity dressed up as lovecraftian fanfiction.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like articles where The Hand tries to have the moral high ground, despite murdering innocent people. Even though the narrative sides against them, it still makes the members in question look cartoonishly callous. That aside, the page itself felt stilted and devoid of content, almost like it was an excuse for the CYOA rather than a proper SCP.

As for the CYOA itself… I didn't like it. The prose could be quite good at times, but the two sets of endings based on the werewolves felt… superfluous. And there was the "two choices lead you to the same place bit". And how much of a non-sequitur the inside world felt. And the worst part for me was how none of the endings really gave a good sense of closure. After exhausting each, all I got was "Welp, this generic eldritch entity won. Good for it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be fair, there’s large swaths of people here who loudly deride religious SCPs if they’re not openly mocking the religion in question.

No one does that. I have no idea why you think this is the case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be fair, there’s large swaths of people here who loudly deride religious SCPs if they’re not openly mocking the religion in question. Those religions have widespread belief irl.

Is it ok for this dude to dislike an article just for a personal belief? He’s depriving himself of some of the better GOC hub work then, where the main character is a Type Blue that’s been thaumaturgically transformed into a biological female. It’s his own loss.

But to be fair (as I outlined below), there’s other flaws about this article outside of that if we’re going to be honest. For some it’s not a deal breaker, but for me, unnatural dialogue and inconsistent character archetypes break it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, the actual SCP entry was disinteresting, the after action report was largely confusing (not in an entertaining way), and the language used was artificial. The non-standard pronouns make no sense coming from a gruff Agent who tells the interviewer to fuck off literally at the end of every page. So for me, the pronouns in a vacuum wouldn’t have done much of anything to detract, but in combination with the other flaws, it was the nail in the coffin. It was a very artificial insertion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woo, Choose Your Own Incident Report :) … can't believe it took me this long to discover it.

And when is a happy ending not a happy ending? Well… "…" :-o

Gotta admit I didn't quite understand what Type Blue was supposed to be at first though. I suppose it would have been difficult to put a footnote in the CYOIR part to explain it, but would there be any way to include a mention in the "main" article that the incident report involved Task Force Whatever (forgive my terrible memory) including former SH member Agent [REDACTED] and assisted by Type Blue [footnote: magic user, colloquially "mage"/"witch"/"wizard"] operative Jann Ekthingy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's probably one of the stronger reasons that the terms haven't received wider acceptance, because they sound rather like using the existing ones with a low-comedy foreign affect. There's no particular need they have to be uncomfortable mutations of the familiar words. And whilst singular they/them has some arguments in its favour - more familiar and easily pronounced so doesn't seem ridiculous or forced, and has a mirror in e.g. German where the equivalent is used rather more routinely as a neuter singular and/or plural pronoun - it does have the singular/plural confusion problem and is, ultimately, just a historical accident that has no logical advantage other than premacy. (I'll admit it's the one I feel more comfortable with, but I'd rather it wasn't that way)

But then, what would we work anyway? Who gets to decide? Seems that the people who it would most directly affect - i.e. those who are the subject of address using the terms - are probably the best to decide. And in this case, that includes the author of the work under discussion.

Either way, completely giving up on a innovative, interesting and well-presented incident report that has a number of "wham" twist endings because of a couple of two/three letter terms of personal address that you personally find laughable even in the face of their gradually increasing RL usage seems… well, just kinda spiteful and phobic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honest question: If "wizard" is typically used for male-identifying magic users, and "witch" for female identifying… what's the term for neuter/bigender/whatever third-gender is implied by the nonbinary pronouns?

(I expect probably we take the Final Fantasy route and use "Mage"?)

Personal note, it's been (mumble) years already, how come all the available alternative pronoun choices are still so unwieldy or unnatural sounding? Or is that just the side effect of unfamiliarity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gonna mirror sometaters and admit that I stopped after encountering the ze/zir thing. I thought it was an effect of the anomaly screwing with his mind but apparently it's a genderfluid thing. Plus, trying to use ze reminds of a very bad French accent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote for the After Action Report.

I'm actually kind of sad that the AAR can't be upvoted as a stand alone article due to format problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what's wrong with it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lemme tell you a story.

My real name irl is Chris. My legal name is Christopher. Anyone who knows me, knows that calling me Christopher will get you on my shitlist pretty fast. I don't particularly like it, or respond to it.

I was having a conversation with a friend, where they were complaining about remembering/using pronouns for someone.

Him: "Why can't they just use the ones they were born with?"

Me: "Hey man, what's my name?"

Him: "Chris?"

Me: "Yeah, what's my full name?"

Him: "Christopher? I guess?"

Me: "And, you know that I don't like to be called Christopher, right?"

Him: "Yeah, you hate it. You said it reminds you of your step-mom being a dick when you were a kid."

Me: "Right, so you don't call me Christopher. You call me Chris, 'cause I asked you to right?"

Him: "Well yeah, it'd be a real dick move to call you something that bothers you and you don't like to be—" pauses for a second "Ahh. Okay, I didn't think about it that way."

Keep this in mind next time you want to get salty about using the words we prefer for ourselves, because all your saying is "being a dick to you is way easier than just making a bare-minimum effort to call you what you prefer."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, how dare people choose the arbitrary words they want to be referred to by! As we all know, language is holy and sacred and cannot be altered or changed by anyone! New words are unheard of!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So basically what you’re saying here is that, In the Future, to secure your upvote, writers should avoid having any characters who aren’t cis? Should we avoid gay people in the future too? Definitely wanna make sure I keep the bigot vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got a laugh out of me for the CYOA when you say they called in the strike team twice, but stopped reading almost immediately when the whole zie/ze thing started popping up. Can't be bothered with that sort of crap.

item.title: Re: OH GODS I AM A RIDICULOUS PERSON
item.content:encoded: Because of this, that thing that lets you to "back to the beginning right before you can make choices" doesn't work properly so I got confused when using it. I think I got all 12 of them.

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: These edits are fine by me. Go ahead and implement them. Thank you :)

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edit
- Hide

To link to the mentioned task forces, I propose the following edits:

The remains of SCP-2975 are monitored by Observation Post SYN-Alpha-019, staffed by members of Mobile Task Force Sigma-3 ("Bibliographers").

to

The remains of SCP-2975 are monitored by Observation Post SYN-Alpha-019, staffed by members of Mobile Task Force Sigma-3 ("Bibliographers").

and

However, the ritual event was terminated, and SCP-2975 was demolished by Mobile Task Force Psi-7 ("Home Improvement").

to

However, the ritual event was terminated, and SCP-2975 was demolished by Mobile Task Force Psi-7 ("Home Improvement").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just plain didn't enjoy the story being told. None of the particular imagery struck a chord with me, and the premise/plot of the CYOA didn't seem like much of an innovation on the tropes found therein.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of fun CYOA but… Iunno, I felt like it was bad use of CYOA stuff in an SCP article. I feel like an SCP-format CYOA should be smoother, I guess? I'm not sure how to eloquently describe why I was unhappy with the use of CYOA here. Sorry about that, you deserve better than a shrug. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite enjoyed the choose your own adventure "Another Sun" report. I couldn't help going back and forth to read everything and was disappointed in the end merely because there was no more. Good job.

I also really enjoyed the idea of another world. My only question is if there's a hole in the sky or a ceiling. I imagine it as the former, this being a CK event and that this other world isn't in ours but somewhere else. Hope I'm right. I'm still trying to wrap my head around the scenarios.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first SCP I read after returning from my most recent hiatus, and an instant favorite. The way the CYOA is tied in to actual CK-shifts is very clever, and conveys the agent's fractured memories very well.

Enthusiastically upvoted to 100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was just looking for this and discovered SCP-2108 is also called another sun

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found the haunted house as a local monument that people were tolerating to be a refreshingly original take on that kind of thing, and the cyoa was just icing on the cake. A well-deserved +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, thanks. Yeah I just found this on the 'most recently created' and jumped in blind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Type Blue" is GOC code for a wizard. This is explained both in the GOC supplement on applied thaumatology and the Sigma-3 magic orientation that was part of the same series as this article. This probably assumes you've read the latter at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just have to add my two cents here. I actually understood Jann to be a member of a different species or in some other way different (my mind went to 'oh, a member of some mage organisation SCP makes use of that's sort of otherworldly in nature, that's cool'). The reasoning that I'd never really seen ze/zir been used as an RL gender-pronoun but also because ze had various powers that is not typically associated with the SCP stuff I've read, plus was described as a Type Blue, which sounds like the kind of vernacular a military organisation might use for otherworldly aid they're making use of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In general, there's a level of writing ability here that makes this piece hard to criticise. I stayed with it through the whole thing, even the CYOA. I actually enjoyed the way the CYOA actually felt more like broken recollection more than a direct "Do X/Do Y" choice.

However…

+ show block
– hide block

One thing that really pops out to me is that there's information about the house in those logs and the addenda that appear pertinent to the description of the SCP that aren't present in the description section. To read the description in isolation, one might even feel that it isn't actually the house, but the subterranean crevasse that is anomalous, as it's the only thing described there as possessing a concretely anomalous effect (ie. disappearing following the destruction of the house and becoming inaccessible).

This is despite the fact that the house itself is revealed to eat people in an immediately subsequent addendum. I would imagine that such pertinent information would need to be included in the description of the anomaly, being the primary source of anomalous effect tied specifically to the house beside being inexplicably popular with the locals. It also presents an immediate risk to the life of anyone who enters, so it would seem reasonable to include there as well.

Overall, I enjoyed it +1

item.title: Re: OH GODS I AM A RIDICULOUS PERSON
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

So is the other set mixed up or not? the ones that are basically the same sequence of events except you forgot to chat with the werewolves? Also…

Does the White Sun have anything to do with the Moon? The Hunter being a giant wolf and the purpose it imbues you with, the werewolves that are warning you about the White Sun (Did the Hunter have anything to do with their condition? saving them from a worse fate?), werewolves turning at the full moon, wolves thought to howl at the moon (and possibly implicitly keeping an eye on it)…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for your thoughts! That was an interesting read.

Having heard your reasoning, I'm satisfied that your use of ze/zir would make sense in-universe; however, it might not be a bad idea to address this briefly somewhere in the text, as you mentioned (something about it being a way to blend in with the Hand).

isn't being politically correct (or even 'respectful', for that matter, since usually in this case it's more an issue of respecting another person than being PC)

I agree with you here. Actually, re-reading my comment, I'm not even sure why I used "politically correct" in the first place - aside from being, as you pointed out, a poor choice of words, it doesn't even convey what I was trying to say (I think I'm too tired…). Allow me to rephrase this better: what I meant was that I thought the narrator, being in a distressed state of mind, would have difficulty not mixing up the pronouns while talking (you mention that difficulty yourself further down), and, being rather rude of nature, wouldn't really care enough to make an effort.

the nameless, genderless narrator

… Are you kidding me!? How the hell did I miss this? I was sure the text mentioned he was a man, how weird… (Of course, this makes it even more plausible that the narrator would be familiar with the use of ze/zir…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show thoughts
- Hide them

What I was trying to get across, through repetition, is that the narrator has too many contradictory memories of what happened and is also forgetting things, so… repeats some things over again, without even realizing it. The CYOA, to me, takes place several times, with key differences each time. The narrator is trying to resolve the contradictions by trying to force all the narrative strings to come together in a similar way.

So, if I understand correctly…every permutation of the narrator's story is actually told within a single interview? Kinda neat. I would definitely emphasize this, as well as the fact that he keeps forgetting, it would help justify the CYOA format. Really though, the way you're using CYOA isn't CYOA in the strictest sense - there's isn't any choosing involved, since you're meant to read every permutation, because they all happened and are all equally valid. That's a very interesting use of the format.

(By the way, I can see now that everything you're telling me here was already more or less in the article, it simply wasn't all that clear to me when I first read it.)

It's also an effect of the cultists trying to 'tweak' things via CK-class reality shifts. If you go too far with that, you risk undoing the entire ritual, is the idea. So the shifts have to be more low-key to make sure a lot of the same stuff still happens. (But they can't control the exact outcome.)

I'm really intrigued by this idea… I thought the shifts were just accidental, this adds a whole new layer (layers?) to the story, and could even help flesh out the cultists a little. I'd definitely love to hear more of this if you happen to make changes to the article, and would reconsider my vote accordingly. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this comment pretty interesting because it goes to something I thought through a lot before writing. And now get to talk about it!

I found it hard to believe that a hardened MTF member, made to recount the most traumatic event of his life, would have the wherewithal to do so in such a politically-correct manner.

Sure, this makes sense — except you're missing that the nameless, genderless narrator (hehe) isn't being politically correct (or even 'respectful', for that matter, since usually in this case it's more an issue of respecting another person than being PC). If they were being politically correct, the pronouns would've been dropped, I agree. The narrator is just used to calling Agent Jann Ekua by the correct pronouns, and used to using correct pronouns in general because of Sigma-3 training to blend in with Hand types.

More in-depth comments
– hide block

Speaking from experience — both I and my real-life live-in partner are non-binary and use alternate pronouns — that's very difficult at first (even if you are non-binary it's not like you magically automatically use correct pronouns with no difficulty) and then gets easier after a few months of practice. (Still fuck it up in contexts which you're not used to, but that's another story.)

Also: Sigma-3 is the team most likely to be 'ahead of the curve' on this, because they have to deal with people outside the Foundation and 'blend in' with them, and refusing to use correct pronouns due to, say, a military background or traditional beliefs or whatnot, is a good way to fuck yourself over real fast.

(The Hand seem the GOI by far most likely to use alternative pronouns, at least in some factions; ironically they also contain members who would probably hate that more than anyone! This is based on real-life experience with pagan groups…. some of them are very open-minded and cool about things, others obsess about male & female energy and get mad about non-binary/trans people.)

Put another way: they are literally trained to use correct pronouns, because it's part of their cover. In our narrator's case, it's become second nature because the training has worked. It's not so different from 'always speaking in another language if you're trained to'.

If you really have your heart set on gender-less pronouns, though, might I suggest they/them instead of ze/zir?

I usually choose ze/zir over they/them because then you don't get singular/plural confusion. Plus, in sci-fi and fantasy works in particular, singular 'they' can be more difficult — because you could really have a gestalt intelligence which isn't non-binary but is a 'they', and therefore give the impression that non-binary people are non-human gestalt intelligences.

(Incidentally, that's also why I personally use ze/zir. I don't really care which alternative pronouns people use for me, but ze/zir tends to be easiest.)

I do wonder if it would be interesting to have the narrator comment on that. It would be realistic enough for the interviewer, who might not have Sigma-3 field training, to mess it up, and have the narrator (who hates the interview and therefore the interviewer) correct it. Probably rudely. Unsure about this, though…

– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some comments!
– hide block

Even the ending does this Lovecraft thing where the hero blacks out just as things are about to get really good, and we never get to know what really happened.

Fun fact: I literally considered directly adapting a Lovecraft story for this. That's not what I did (I did partially pastiche the kid's Choose Your Own Adventure book "The Underground Kingdom" and the trope of a house that eats people, though — for the record — I've never seen Monster House), but that sort of Thing was intentional.

That doesn't make it good, though. Now that we can make major edits, I'm tempted to clarify more of what's going on. Because, well, I know exactly what was going on, and it's a shame that the implications don't really carry through so much from

since most of the permutations simply offer a different way of getting to the same place, thereby diminishing the overall impact of the narrative through repetition.

What I was trying to get across, through repetition, is that the narrator has too many contradictory memories of what happened and is also forgetting things, so… repeats some things over again, without even realizing it. The CYOA, to me, takes place several times, with key differences each time. The narrator is trying to resolve the contradictions by trying to force all the narrative strings to come together in a similar way.

It's also an effect of the cultists trying to 'tweak' things via CK-class reality shifts. If you go too far with that, you risk undoing the entire ritual, is the idea. So the shifts have to be more low-key to make sure a lot of the same stuff still happens. (But they can't control the exact outcome.)

Unfortunately, I'm not sure that device worked… so I may change some of that in the edits. (Of course, some of the issue was just the extreme time constraints this had to be written under.)

But if you simply reworked this as a traditional exploration log

Unfortunately, I'd lose out on the benefits of the CYOA (I also think the format is just fun and likely wouldn't have written this otherwise), and any attempt to portray the effects of a CK-class reality shift. That said, I think I will make some alterations nonetheless.

– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't going to comment on this, since it's just a small nit-pick I have, but seeing as we're discussing the gender-less pronouns, I have to say they kind of broke the immersion for me, personally - simply put, I found it hard to believe that a hardened MTF member, made to recount the most traumatic event of his life, would have the wherewithal to do so in such a politically-correct manner. You'd think the use of preferred pronouns would be the last thing on his mind at that moment. And I remind you, this is a guy who isn't afraid to offend people, having no qualms about telling his interviewer to fuck off in every single ending. :)

If you really have your heart set on gender-less pronouns, though, might I suggest they/them instead of ze/zir? I don't know if there's a huge difference between those two sets of pronouns from a non-binary person's point-of-view, but to me at least this would feel a lot more realistic, coming from a guy like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really, really torn on this one…

+ Thoughts
- Hide

On the one hand, I'm awed by the amount of work that went into this - seriously, your output for the contest has been simply staggering, I can't imagine how you managed it all. The ending of the story, inside the pit, was also really out there, in a way I haven't seen on the site before, which was pretty bold on your part. And the way you wrote the interview logs, in this incredibly, disturbingly believable manner, makes them my favorite part of the story.

However, I felt that the characters, both protagonists and antagonists, weren't well-defined enough to engage me, and parts of the plots felt somewhat generic in an Arkham Horror-y sort of way (team of heroes in an evil-infested town fighting off zombie-like villagers as they work their way to a haunted house sitting on a portal to hell). Likewise, while the story does pick up when they get to the cave, the cultists never feel like anything more than your typical Cthulhu cultists. Even the ending does this Lovecraft thing where the hero blacks out just as things are about to get really good, and we never get to know what really happened.

Most of all, though, I think the CYOA doesn't really serve any purpose here, or accomplish anything. Don't get me wrong, it's a gutsy and creative move, and I think it's very clever the way you justify its use in-universe, but having read every permutation and ending to the story, I didn't feel my experience enriched in any way by it - quite the opposite, I felt it was diluted as a result, since most of the permutations simply offer a different way of getting to the same place, thereby diminishing the overall impact of the narrative through repetition. You clearly have one permutation that's better than the others (by which I mean the one where the heroes do stuff, instead of trying to stay out of danger), but it's being seriously weighed down by the CYOA format.

Let me emphasize that: I think most of the problems I outlined with the story could be fixed if you simply dropped the CYOA format, and focused instead on fleshing out a single narrative thread. As this stands, you can't go into the details of the plot, of the settings, of the characters, or build up as much tension as you could, because you're forced to keep things simple so they'll fit in the CYOA format. But if you simply reworked this as a traditional exploration log, fleshing out both the heroes and the villains more, their interactions with each other, their fear, focusing on the action and the horror in a more in-depth way, I could see this making for an epic, terrifying read, à la SCP-610. All the elements are already there, it's just the execution that I find lacking. If you could just instill the same realism, the same believability, the same level of characterization from the interview logs into the rest of the story…wow.

Of course, this is just my opinion - feel free to disagree. In its current form, though, while it certainly isn't bad, the article just isn't quite doing it for me.

No-voting, (though certainly leaning more toward an upvote than a downvote).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this comment pretty interesting, and it made me think a bit.

as a standalone work I found it didn't really seem to do much work in terms of making clear what was going on or what the significance of any of it was.

and the events inside the hollow earth just seemed divorced from any context.

I wish the focus had been kept there rather than on the CYOA sort-of-Tale,

The SCP and the Tale are very much related thematically, meant to be two sides of the same coin, but I don't believe even a single reader has seen that. Which, of course, is my own fault (I think my work in this contest was less thematically successful than my work for the GOI Contest, though I can partially blame the time constraints), and something I'll look to address in the now-inevitable next update.

Though, also of course, a lot of this is baked in. The CYOA isn't gonna drastically change, just get expanded / some connections clarified. Hopefully I can at least provide more context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this is, for the most part, a tale masquerading as an article, and judging the CYOA as a tale I'm not sure I'm really that interested in it. Lots of things happen, certainly, but as a standalone work I found it didn't really seem to do much work in terms of making clear what was going on or what the significance of any of it was. Most of what happened in the house was styled in a way that seemed to gloss over it and prevented any of the images from landing to much effect, and the events inside the hollow earth just seemed divorced from any context.

I'll admit I had some trouble with the piece on a generic level - I'm rather biased against pieces of the Foundationverse that lurch too far towards urban fantasy in the more narrow sense, and this story set off several of those alarms with the giant Hunters and werewolves and so on. The "It all started with the werewolves" line also felt off, and felt like it was trying too hard to achieve a certain tone which didn't really gel with the rest of the work.

There are things I liked quite a lot here but they were almost all in the interviews rather than the CYOA. I particularly liked the reticent admittance that the magical community fucked up big time in dealing with the Memory House. I actually think that interview might be one of the better pieces of political satire on the wiki, as an allegory for the way in which counter-cultural, marginalised, or anti-authority communities sometimes end up turning a blind eye to totally appalling behaviour among respected members of the 'in-group' out of misplaced comradeship. I wish the focus had been kept there rather than on the CYOA sort-of-Tale, because that didn't really hold my interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gi!

(Inupiat expression of agreement, encouragement, and the like!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's as far as you can get, but now that you say that, I'm tempted to write past that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You weren't being explicit? I mean, I read both documents in one go, so that probably helped, but the connections were pretty clear to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: is there any route in the AAR past the giant wolf or is that just as far as you can get?

item.title: Re: OH GODS I AM A RIDICULOUS PERSON
item.content:encoded:
ARGH. Very perceptive of you. As much as it would be funny to say "ohohoho but perhaps you just missed something because I am just so clever" that is, indeed, very much a bug. And, worse, a bug I checked for repeatedly because I'd fucked it up early on. And considering that's the only real "happy" ending if you pay close attention…

Thank you! I will go in and fix that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It feels like an article that deserves to either be beefed up a little before getting to the CYOA or changed to a format more suited to it.

My intent was to make them two stand-alone complementary pieces; peoples' reactions seem to imply that the SCP section doesn't quite work with that.

I intend to expand it a little bit post-contest. Not a massive rewrite, just adding some examination of the house's "POV" and its history, which presently is only implied in a sideways fashion in the AAR and the interviews.

Post-contest I may try and embed the Twine file in the SCP document itself, too.

I'm happy you liked the CYOA! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was particularly pleased to find out what the White Hand was, not to mention I have a feeling that first interviewed person was one of the authors of that document.

Man, in that case, I might put the explicit link to the White Hand back. (That fell to one of the streamlining edits.) Unless I still do have another explicit reference in there that I forgot about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a bit put off when the AAR opened up in a new tab, but obviously now know that was unavoidable, so no issues there. I bellowed laughing when I realised you were doing a CYOA here - it's an excellent way to present a reality-restructuring event, and novel formats done well seriously boost my interest in any skip.

I guess the only issue I have with this is the simple fact it's presented as a skip instead of a simple AAR. The surrounding containment procedures and description are fairly sparse, though the interviews are nice and give some good flavour to the town outside of the sort of generic "townsfolk taken over by eldrich monstrosity" thing. It feels like an article that deserves to either be beefed up a little before getting to the CYOA or changed to a format more suited to it.

+1 overall, though. The CYOA really is awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The CYOA was better than in that Hand article, more engaging over all, but when I see CYOAs, I want to zip through them quickly so I can see all the branches. That wasn't happening with this one, and I'm sorry to say I didn't finish it. That's more a problem with me than the article, though, and there's plenty here for an upvote. I was particularly pleased to find out what the White Hand was, not to mention I have a feeling that first interviewed person was one of the authors of that document. This is a good ending for this group. :)

item.title: Re: OH GODS I AM A RIDICULOUS PERSON
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

There appears to be some epilogue mismatch in the "…4" set. Namely, the ending where your character says "I'm not scared, I'm staring right back"(paraphrased) seems like it would fit great with the epilogue with "Stop watching" in it. But it appears to actually lead to the epilogue with "you don't deserve it" (also paraphrased) which seems like it would fit better with the ending where you shoot the Hunter. I presume the other two are mismatched in a similar manner. Is this a bug, or am I missing something?

Full disclosure: I'm poking around in the source, since my browser doesn't like Twine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for the late reply, but the article feels a lot smoother now. I really like it; great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two things I loved about this:
One, the idea of a town harbouring a human-eating house and a doomsday cult because "it's tradition." It's weirdly believable.
Two, the way you presented reality restructuring events. Imagining it as a CYOA story where all the paths (sort of) happened, not just the one you chose, is a great angle to come at it from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This is my favorite type of scip. A good reality bender mixed with an even better Choose Your Own Adventure makes me wish I could vote more than once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, this was entertaining.

I liked the CYOA bit that was here better than in "Conspiracy," mainly because this was actually a narrative and not a abstract bit of snippets. Additionally, the narrator was pretty hilarious throughout the entire ordeal.

+1.

item.title: OH GODS I AM A RIDICULOUS PERSON
item.content:encoded:
So… Major update. To the After Action Report. (Also, submitted literally seconds before Decibelle closed the editing period, what is wrong with me?)

  • The reader can now take an entirely different path by simply not taking an early action, in a way that was logical, but wasn't originally a choice. This will result in a different set of endings.
  • The reader can also make a different key decision at the very end; again, one that was logical, but not originally available. (You can now "land" rather than "exit" or "enter". Hopefully that's not too spoilery!)
  • There are now a total of 12 different endings instead of 3. (Full disclosure, some elements are duplicated. Tried to make them sufficiently different, though at least one pair are identical up until the hidden epilogue. Feedback welcome.)
  • All the "secret epilogues" are tweaked. (If you didn't find them, well, now you know they're there.)
  • Changed a few other things. Small edits throughout.

Same deal as before — I'll make any edits for spelling or grammar or accidental bugs. (edit: now done two rounds; unaltered file still uploaded just in case). Any new major edits will need to wait til the contest closes. But I'm pretty damn happy with what's here and don't currently anticipate further major changes.

(I'll announce in the update thread tomorrow when people are actually awake!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, it seems simple at first but is very deeply layered in fairly gonzo way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers
GODDAMN BABY WEREWOLVES AWW YISS

It makes me happy that this exists in the SCP-verse. It's adorable, and makes me wonder if they were all born as a single litter. And if they shape-shifted as babies so you could snuggle a whole armful of baby werewolves d'awwwwwwww and okay I'm done now you all saw nothing.

GODDAMN BABY WEREWOLVES AWW YISS

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a Public History Grad student, so the whole "destruction of a Landmark" thing hits pretty close to home for me. While I agree that it's a good thing that the article sorta took a backseat to the mini-Tale AAR twine-thing, I wanted more investigation into the House.

The Tale was good. Although kinda here-and-there narrative-wise, it was an entertaining piece that could have probably been its own document.

I say again that if there was more to the "original article," I'd enjoy it a whole lot. The idea that the Foundation has to consider its effects on existing historical and cultural resources is cool to me—or maybe that's just my History Nerd-ness showing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed the base article, actually. I like interview logs (no way to tell that from my articles roster, nosiree), and I found these interview logs enjoyable and a good example of what this MTF contributes to worldbuilding.

Another Sun was mostly enjoyable, but I found the experience a bit uneven, and the CYOA format, in the way it's presented, took me out of it a bit (because it's not me in the story, I consciously make choices for what I think would be interesting, not what I think would be good for the MTF. As a result, I'm reminded of my out-of-universe status with every choice.). As such, I feel the CYOA doesn't work as well as it did in "Conspiracy". One other nitpick would be that, despite mentioning CK reconfigurations in the beginning, the route I took never mentioned them in the actual course of things. My ending (descend into the void, enter the white sun) also seemed to end rather abruptly.

All in all, though, it was enjoyable, and the writing was generally good. I hope we can get some more Sigma-3 stories in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If there is an issue with the AAR, I have personally decided that it is legitimate and will count within the context of this contest as fufilling one of the requirements. The AARs have been done in really creative and clever ways, with no one sticking to one specific format (and the AARs ranging from a supplement to a tale to a tale-supplement hybrid to different kind of storytelling, etc.). This was one of the more clever ways I've seen it accomplished, and it will count as the requirement for the AAR.
